# 🚗 Autonomous Highway Driving with PPO
### CMP4501 – Applied Reinforcement Learning | Bahçeşehir University

> **Option A — Autonomous Driving with Highway-Env**

---

| Field | Details |
|---|---|
| **Course** | CMP4501 – Introduction to AI and Expert Systems |
| **Instructor** | Dr. Günet Eroğlu |
| **Team Members** | Kerem Özdemir, Emir Filik |

| **Algorithm** | Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) |
| **Library** | `stable-baselines3` |

## 🎬 Training Evolution

> The video below shows the agent's progression across three training checkpoints: untrained, half-trained, and fully trained.

[▶ Watch Full Evolution Video](videos/stage3_full.mp4)
---

## 📐 Methodology

### 2.1 — The Reward Function

The core challenge in this project is that we are dealing with a **multi-objective optimization problem**: the agent must simultaneously pursue speed, stay in its lane, and avoid collisions. A single-objective reward (e.g., pure speed) is not enough — without a collision penalty, the agent simply accelerates and crashes instantly.

We defined the following composite reward signal:

$$R_t = \alpha \cdot v_t - \beta \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{collision}_t] + \gamma \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{right lane}_t] - \delta \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{lane change}_t]$$

| Symbol | Type | Role |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha \cdot v_t$ | **Speed reward** | $v_t \in [0, 1]$ is the normalized vehicle speed. Encourages the agent to drive faster rather than stopping or idling to avoid traffic. |
| $\beta \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{collision}_t]$ | **Collision penalty** | A hard binary penalty applied when the agent crashes into another vehicle. This term dominates all others during early training to teach basic survival. |
| $\gamma \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{right lane}_t]$ | **Right-lane bonus** | A small bonus for occupying the rightmost or second-rightmost lane, which reflects real-world highway etiquette and prevents the agent from drifting into fast lanes unnecessarily. |
| $\delta \cdot \mathbb{1}[\text{lane change}_t]$ | **Lane-change penalty** | A small cost applied each time the agent performs a lateral maneuver. Discourages erratic weaving behavior without completely forbidding lane changes when needed. |

**Why this function works:** The four terms create a natural priority hierarchy. Surviving (avoiding $\beta$) is the most urgent objective; then moving fast (maximizing $\alpha \cdot v_t$); then behaving cleanly (earning $\gamma$, avoiding $\delta$). Without the lane-change penalty in particular, early agents would oscillate between lanes endlessly since the speed reward alone does not punish that behavior.

---

### 2.2 — The Model

We used **Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO)** from the `stable-baselines3` library with an `MlpPolicy` (multi-layer perceptron).

**Why PPO?**
PPO is a well-established on-policy actor-critic algorithm. Unlike DQN, it directly optimizes a stochastic policy and handles continuous or high-dimensional action feedback naturally. More practically, PPO is reliable and stable with moderate hyperparameter tuning — a significant advantage in a semester project where iteration time is limited. DQN was considered but tends to be more sensitive to replay buffer size and target network update frequency, which adds tuning overhead.

**Why `MlpPolicy`?**
The `highway-env` environment provides **kinematic feature vectors** as observations (positions, velocities of nearby vehicles), not raw pixel images. There is no benefit to using a CNN here — an MLP processes the structured numerical input directly and trains significantly faster.

**Key Hyperparameters:**

| Hyperparameter | Value | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| `learning_rate` | `3e-4` | Standard PPO default; stable convergence without overshooting |
| `n_steps` | `2048` | Rollout buffer length per update; long enough to capture multi-step dependencies in traffic |
| `batch_size` | `64` | Mini-batch size for gradient updates; balances compute and gradient variance |
| `n_epochs` | `10` | Number of passes over each rollout; more passes = better sample efficiency |
| `gamma` | `0.99` | Discount factor; high value to account for long episode horizons |
| `gae_lambda` | `0.95` | GAE smoothing parameter; reduces variance in advantage estimation |
| `ent_coef` | `0.01` | Entropy bonus to prevent premature policy collapse and encourage continued exploration |
| `clip_range` | `0.2` | PPO clipping threshold; core stability mechanism of the algorithm |

**Network Architecture:** Two hidden layers of 256 units each with `tanh` activations (SB3 default for `MlpPolicy`). Separate actor and critic heads share the MLP trunk.

---

### 2.3 — States and Actions

**Observation Space — Kinematic Features:**

The agent observes a `5 × 5` kinematic matrix at each timestep. Each row corresponds to a vehicle (ego vehicle + 4 nearest neighbors), and the columns are:

| Feature | Description |
|---|---|
| `x` | Longitudinal position (normalized) |
| `y` | Lateral position / lane index (normalized) |
| `vx` | Longitudinal velocity |
| `vy` | Lateral velocity |
| `presence` | Binary flag — is this vehicle actually present? |

This gives the agent a structured, compact representation of its local traffic context. Absent vehicles are masked via the `presence` flag, so the agent is not confused by empty slots.

**Action Space — Discrete (5 actions):**

| Index | Action |
|---|---|
| 0 | **Lane Left** — move one lane to the left |
| 1 | **Idle** — maintain current speed and lane |
| 2 | **Lane Right** — move one lane to the right |
| 3 | **Faster** — increase target speed |
| 4 | **Slower** — decrease target speed |

The discrete action space was kept as-is from the environment defaults. No custom action masking was applied — the agent learns on its own which actions are available in context.

---

## 📊 Training Analysis

### 3.1 — Reward Graph

<img src="data:image/jpeg;base64," alt="Training Performance – Reward vs Episodes" width="900"/>

*Figure 1 — 10-episode rolling mean reward over 1,050 training episodes.*

---

### 3.2 — Commentary

The training curve tells a clear story with three distinct phases.

**Phase 1 — Chaotic Exploration (Episodes 0–300):**
The rolling mean stays mostly between 6 and 12 with extremely high variance. The agent has not yet learned a consistent survival policy — it explores randomly and crashes frequently. The sharp dip to around 4 near episode 200 is characteristic of a temporary behavioral collapse: the agent stumbled into a local strategy that happened to work for a few episodes (perhaps staying slow and idle), then abandoned it as the policy updated, causing a temporary regression before finding a better direction.

**Phase 2 — Rapid Improvement (Episodes 300–700):**
This is the steepest learning phase. The rolling mean climbs from ~10 up to around 18–19, with the agent visibly gaining competence around episode 400. This likely corresponds to the point where the collision penalty starts being reliably avoided and the agent begins optimizing for speed rather than pure survival. Variance remains high because the agent is still actively adjusting its lane-change and speed strategy, but the floor of bad episodes keeps rising.

**Phase 3 — Convergence and Stabilization (Episodes 700–1050):**
The reward stabilizes in the 17–22 range. The agent has found a largely consistent high-reward policy. The fluctuations that remain are inherent to the stochastic traffic environment — identical policies produce different rewards depending on where traffic spawns. A brief dip around episode 900 suggests the optimizer briefly tested a slightly different policy before reverting, which is typical PPO behavior near convergence.

**Overall:** The learning curve shows healthy monotonic improvement without reward hacking or divergence. The absence of any catastrophic collapse after episode 300 suggests the composite reward function was well-calibrated.

---

## 🔧 Challenges and Failures

### The "Frozen Car" Problem

Early on, after the first hundred or so training episodes, we noticed that the agent had learned a very particular strategy: **do nothing**. It would set itself to the slowest possible speed, hug the rightmost lane, and refuse to change lanes under any circumstances. Technically it was earning the right-lane bonus and avoiding collisions, but the speed reward was essentially zero. The reward per episode was hovering around 6–8 — mathematically positive, but completely useless as a driving agent.

The root cause was that our original $\beta$ (collision penalty) was set too aggressively at `-20`, while $\alpha$ (speed reward) was only `0.4`. The risk-reward tradeoff was so skewed that any movement felt "dangerous" to the agent. It had correctly solved *its* version of the problem — maximize the function as defined — just not the version we intended.

We fixed this in two steps. First, we reduced the collision penalty to `-5` and increased the speed coefficient to `1.0`, rebalancing the tradeoff so that a crash is expensive but not catastrophically so relative to the speed gain. Second, we added the lane-change penalty ($\delta = 0.1$) to stop the agent from swinging to the opposite extreme — when the collision penalty was reduced, a new agent started weaving aggressively between lanes to find gaps. The small lane-change cost smoothed this out. After retraining from scratch with the updated weights, the agent began showing meaningful improvement by episode 150 and produced the curve visible above.

---

## 🗂 Repository Structure

```
cmp4501-highway-ppo/
│
├── README.md
├── requirements.txt
│
├── src/
│   ├── train.py          # Main training loop
│   ├── evaluate.py       # Evaluation + video recording
│   ├── config.py         # All hyperparameters in one place
│   └── utils.py          # Reward wrapper, helpers
│
├── assets/
│   ├── evolution.gif     # Training evolution video
│   └── reward_plot.png   # Reward curve
│
└── videos/
    ├── stage1_untrained.mp4
    ├── stage2_half.mp4
    └── stage3_full.mp4
```

---

## ⚙️ Quickstart

```bash
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/keremozdemir/cmp4501-highway-ppo.git
cd cmp4501-highway-ppo

# 2. Install dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt

# 3. Train the agent
python src/train.py

# 4. Record evaluation video
python src/evaluate.py --checkpoint models/ppo_stage3.zip
```

**Requirements:** Python 3.10+, `stable-baselines3`, `highway-env`, `gymnasium`, `matplotlib`, `numpy`

---

## 📦 Dependencies

```
gymnasium==0.29.1
highway-env==1.9.1
stable-baselines3==2.3.0
torch==2.2.0
numpy==1.26.4
matplotlib==3.8.4
```
